1: shadow O God, you are my God; earnestly I seek you; my soul thirsts for you; my flesh faints for you, as in a dry and weary land where there is no water. Christ in the wilderness of temptation (Matthew 4:1-11) inhabits this desert thirst — yet he is also the one who satisfies: I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me shall not hunger, and whoever believes in me shall never thirst (John 6:35).
2: revelation of God So I have looked upon you in the sanctuary, beholding your power and glory. The sanctuary where God's glory is seen is Christ himself: we have seen his glory, glory as of the only Son from the Father, full of grace and truth (John 1:14). The Transfiguration is the moment when disciples behold his power and glory in a foretaste of the resurrection (Matthew 17:1-8).
3: shadow Because your steadfast love is better than life, my lips will praise you. For Christ, the Father's steadfast love was literally valued above his own life: I lay down my life (John 10:17-18). Paul's echo — to live is Christ, and to die is gain (Philippians 1:21) — reflects the same calculus. The one who counts love better than life gives his life freely and is raised.
4: shadow So I will bless you as long as I live; in your name I will lift up my hands. Christ's lifted hands at his Ascension: lifting up his hands he blessed them. While he blessed them, he parted from them and was carried up into heaven (Luke 24:50-51). The blessing with lifted hands is his final gesture — the one who praised the Father through his life blesses his people as he departs.
5: shadow My soul will be satisfied as with fat and rich food, and my mouth will praise you with joyful lips, The rich satisfaction of communion with God is realized in the Eucharist — the feast Christ provides where souls are satisfied (John 6:55-58). His mouth praised the Father even at the Last Supper (Matthew 26:30 — when they had sung a hymn, they went out) and from the cross (Luke 23:46).
6: shadow when I remember you upon my bed, and meditate on you in the watches of the night; Christ's night prayers — in these days he went out to the mountain to pray, and all night he continued in prayer to God (Luke 6:12). Gethsemane is the supreme night watch: the Son meditates on the Father through the darkest hours before the Passion.
7: shadow for you have been my help, and in the shadow of your wings I will sing for joy. The shadow of God's wings as shelter: Christ applies this to Jerusalem — How often would I have gathered your children together as a hen gathers her brood under her wings (Matthew 23:37). He longs to be the shelter his people refuse; yet for those who accept him, he is the wing-shadow of the Father's protection.
8: shadow My soul clings to you; your right hand upholds me. Christ's intimate clinging to the Father through the Passion: Father, into your hands I commit my spirit (Luke 23:46). The right hand that upholds is the one that raises — God exalted him at his right hand (Acts 5:31). Upheld through death, he is set at the right hand of the Majesty on high.
9: shadow But those who seek to destroy my life shall go down into the depths of the earth; Those who sought Christ's life — Herod (Matt 2), the Sanhedrin (Matt 26), Judas (Matthew 26:47-50) — face the depths they intended for him. Christ himself descended into the depths of death (Ephesians 4:9 — he also descended into the lower regions) and rose; his enemies have no such resurrection.
10: shadow they shall be given over to the power of the sword; they shall be a portion for jackals. The judgment reserved for those who destroyed the righteous one — Judas (Acts 1:18-19, dying in the field of blood) and Jerusalem (destroyed in AD 70 as Jesus predicted in Matt 24). The one delivered to the sword rose; those who wielded it suffer the consequences.
11: shadow But the king shall rejoice in God; all who swear by him shall exult, for the mouths of liars will be stopped. The king who rejoices is the risen Christ: for the joy that was set before him he endured the cross, despising the shame (Hebrews 12:2). The mouths of liars — the false witnesses at the trial (Matthew 26:59-61) — are stopped by the resurrection, which exposes every false accusation as void.
